title: Doc export
excerpt:
I use board template 
How can i get an ordered output of the date in {{TableStart:Items}}

posts.author: Tamir (Tamir Blajchman)
posts.cookedHtml:
I use board template
How can i get an ordered output of the date in {{TableStart:Items}}

posts.author: Clarey (MondayMotivator)
posts.cookedHtml:
To get an ordered output of the date in a Word or PDF export using {{TableStart:Items}} in a monday.com board template, you need to sort the items by the date column before the data is merged—because the template engine (like the one powered by DocuGen or similar tools) will not sort them dynamically in the Word/PDF template itself.

Here’s how to do it:

Step-by-step solution:

  1. Go to your monday.com board.
  2. Sort the items by the date column:
  • Click the column header of your Date column.
  • Choose “Sort A→Z” or “Sort Z→A” depending on how you want it ordered.
  1. Apply the filter/sort permanently (if needed):
  • In some integrations (like DocuGen, Formstack, or Eledo), only the current sort/filter view is exported.
  • So keep the sort applied when generating the export.
  1. In your Word template, just use your normal:

css

CopyEdit

{{TableStart:Items}}
{{DateColumnName}} - {{OtherColumn}}
{{TableEnd:Items}}

But there is no way to sort directly in the template using Liquid or Mail Merge syntax—the sorting must be done in the board. Let me know with a like if that helps or feel free to ask more

posts.author: elvera33ford (Elvera Ford)
posts.cookedHtml:
Tamir Blajchman:

I use board template mycardstatement

How can i get an ordered output of the date in {{TableStart:Items}}

It sounds like you’re using a templating system, and you want to ensure the dates within a {{TableStart:Items}} block are displayed in a specific order (likely ascending or descending).

Without knowing the exact templating language you’re using (e.g., Jinja2, Liquid, Handlebars, a custom one), I can give you general approaches. The most common way to achieve an ordered output of dates is by sorting the data before it’s passed to the template, or by using a sorting filter/function within the template itself.

Here are the common methods, ordered from most recommended to less recommended:

1. Sort the Data Before Passing to the Template (Recommended)

This is generally the cleanest and most efficient approach. Sort your Items data in your programming language (Python, Ruby, Node.js, PHP, etc.) before you render the template.

Example (Python/Jinja2-like):

Let’s say your Items is a list of dictionaries:

# In your Python code (before rendering the template)
items_data = [
    {"name": "Meeting A", "date": "2025-07-10"},
    {"name": "Event B", "date": "2025-07-05"},
    {"name": "Task C", "date": "2025-07-12"},
]

# Sort by date (assuming 'date' is a string in YYYY-MM-DD format, which sorts lexicographically)
# For more complex date objects, you'd convert them to actual date objects first for accurate sorting.
items_data_sorted = sorted(items_data, key=lambda x: x['date'])

# Pass items_data_sorted to your template
render_template('your_board_template.html', Items=items_data_sorted)

Then in your template, {{TableStart:Items}} would naturally iterate over the already sorted data.

Advantages:

  • Keeps template logic clean.
  • Often more performant, especially for large datasets, as sorting is done in the application layer.
  • Easier to debug.

2. Sort Within the Template Using a Filter/Function

Many templating engines provide a way to sort collections directly within the template. The syntax will vary significantly depending on the engine.

Example (Jinja2-like sort filter):

{{TableStart:Items | sort(attribute='date')}}
    <p>Date: {{ date }}</p>
    <p>Name: {{ name }}</p>
{{TableEnd:Items}}

Example (Liquid-like sort filter):

{% assign sorted_items = Items | sort: 'date' %}
{% for item in sorted_items %}
    <p>Date: {{ item.date }}</p>
    <p>Name: {{ item.name }}</p>
{% endfor %}

Advantages:

  • Self-contained within the template, which can be convenient for simple cases.

Disadvantages:

  • Can make templates harder to read and maintain for complex sorting.
  • Might have performance implications for very large datasets, as sorting happens during template rendering.

3. Custom Logic Within Your {{TableStart:Items}} Loop (Less Recommended)

This is generally not ideal as it relies on the internal implementation of {{TableStart:Items}}, which might not be designed for in-loop sorting. Some very basic templating systems might not offer built-in sorting. In such a rare case, you might need to pre-process the data more extensively.

To help me give you a more precise answer, please tell me:

  • What templating language or system are you using? (e.g., Jinja2, Liquid, Handlebars, a specific CMS’s templating, etc.)
  • What does your Items data structure look like? (e.g., [{"date": "...", "name": "..."}, {...}], or something else?)
  • What is the format of your date string? (e.g., “YYYY-MM-DD”, “MM/DD/YYYY”, etc.)

Once I have this information, I can provide a more tailored and accurate solution.
